coredata.dc:title: Bio-inspired pigment particles with dual-variation modes of structural colors and fluorescence
coredata.dc:creator: Cai, Lijun
Wang, Yu
Xu, Dongyu
Chen, Hanxu
Zhao, Yuanjin
coredata.dc:description: Colorful pigments have demonstrated values in constructing dynamic patterns for different applications. Attempts in this field tend to develop pigments with multiple modes for color variation. Herein, inspired by the color variation mechanism of chameleons, we propose a novel kind of pigments with dual color variations (PDCVs) by integrating hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs) within photonic crystal (PhC) particles. The HCDs with blue dispersion emission and red aggregation-induced emission are generated via one-pot solvothermal treatment. The PDCVs are generated by using HCDs-tagged responsive hydrogel to replicate the nanostructure of silica nanoparticles assembled spherical PhC particles. As the dispersion/aggregation of HCDs and hydrogel lattice of the PhC are depending on their surrounding solvent, the resultant PDCVs can perform dual color variations, including fluorescence and structure color, by changing their solvent. Based on this feature, we have demonstrated that the PDCVs can be employed as elements to construct various dynamic colorful patterns with wide viewing angle and allow for eminent information encryption. There results indicate that the proposed PDCVs are promising to have practical applications in the fields of information encryption, displaying, anti-counterfeiting, and so on.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bio-inspired
Particle
Structural color
Colloidal crystal
Carbon dot

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Color variation mechanism of Chameleons. (b) Schematic illustration of pigments with dual color variations.

(a) Synthesis of VHCDs and their aggregation in water. (b) FT-IR spectra of HCDs. The abbreviation AIE represents aggregation-induced emission. (c) FT-IR spectra of VHCDs. (d) TEM images of VHCDs. Scale bars are 50 nm in (i) and 25 nm in (ii). (e) Images of VHCDs in ethanol under sunlight (i) and UV light(ii), and VHCDs in water under sunlight (iii) and UV light(iv). (f) Fluorescence spectra of VHCDs in ethanol and water.

(a) Schematic illustration of the polymerization process of VHCDs-tagged hydrogel. (b-d) SEM images of the inner structure of PhC particles(b), gel-filled hybrid PhC particles(c) and PDCVs(d), scale bar is 400 nm. (e-f) Optical images of PhC particles(e) and PDCVs(f). (g)Fluorescent images of PDCVs, scale bar is 250 μm. (h) Reflection spectra of PhC particles and PDCVs. (i) The influence of concentration of PEGDA on the swelling rate of the hydrogel.

(a) Fluorescence images of PDCVs after exposing to ethanol solution, scale bar is 100 μm. (b) Optical images of PDCVs exposed to solvent containing difference ethanol/water fraction, scale bar is 100 μm. (c) Fluorescence spectra of PDCVs exposed to solvent containing difference ethanol/water fraction. (d) Refraction spectra of PDCVs exposed to solvent containing difference ethanol/water fraction.

(a) Schematic illustration of construction of dynamic patterns based on the fluorescence variation. (b) Schematic illustration of network of RhB-contained AAm particles after exposing to ethanol. (c) Schematic illustration of network of PDCVs particles after exposing to ethanol. (d) Dynamic patterns based on the fluorescence variation including the initial pattern(i), pattern exposed to ethanol(ii), outline of the “four leaf clover” pattern(iii), partial enlarged view of the pattern(iv). Scale bars are 1800 μm in i-iii and 250 μm in iv, respectively.

(a) Schematic illustration of construction of dynamic patterns based on the structural variation. (b) Optical images of the initial dynamic patterns. (c) Optical images of the dynamic patterns exposed in solvent containing an ethanol/water fraction of 30%. (d) Optical images of the dynamic patterns exposed in solvent containing an ethanol/water fraction of 60%. (e) Optical images of the dynamic patterns exposed in solvent containing an ethanol/water fraction of 90%. Scale bars are 1200 μm in i-ii and 170 μm in iii, respectively.

(a) Schematic illustration of employing PDCVs for information encryption. (b, e) Optical images of the initial dynamic patterns. (c, f) Optical images of the dynamic patterns exposed in solvent containing an ethanol/water fraction of 90%. (d, g) Fluorescence images of dynamic patterns exposed in solvent containing an ethanol/water fraction of 90%. Scale bars is 1600 μm.

head.ce:title: Bio-inspired pigment particles with dual-variation modes of structural colors and fluorescence

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Rheumatology and Immunology, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Rheumatology and Immunology, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Oujiang Laboratory (Zhejiang Lab for Regenerative Medicine, Vision and Brain Health), Wenzhou Institute, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Oujiang Laboratory (Zhejiang Lab for Regenerative Medicine, Vision and Brain Health)
University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Oujiang Laboratory (Zhejiang Lab for Regenerative Medicine, Vision and Brain Health), Wenzhou Institute, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Wenzhou, Zhejiang 325001, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Biomedicine Innovation Center, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Biomedicine Innovation Center, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Rheumatology and Immunology, Nanjing Drum Tower Hospital, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 210096, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we have proposed a novel pigment with fluorescence variation and structure color variation by integrating carbon dots with photonic crystals. Both the fluorescence variation and structure color variation could be employed to construct various dynamic patterns. Furthermore, the combination of the dual color variation exhibited prominent performance in complex information encryption with convenient and fast decryption method.

Colorful pigments have demonstrated values in constructing dynamic patterns for different applications. Attempts in this field tend to develop pigments with multiple modes for color variation. Herein, inspired by the color variation mechanism of chameleons, we propose a novel kind of pigments with dual color variations (PDCVs) by integrating hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs) within photonic crystal (PhC) particles. The HCDs with blue dispersion emission and red aggregation-induced emission are generated via one-pot solvothermal treatment. The PDCVs are generated by using HCDs-tagged responsive hydrogel to replicate the nanostructure of silica nanoparticles assembled spherical PhC particles. As the dispersion/aggregation of HCDs and hydrogel lattice of the PhC are depending on their surrounding solvent, the resultant PDCVs can perform dual color variations, including fluorescence and structure color, by changing their solvent. Based on this feature, we have demonstrated that the PDCVs can be employed as elements to construct various dynamic colorful patterns with wide viewing angle and allow for eminent information encryption. There results indicate that the proposed PDCVs are promising to have practical applications in the fields of information encryption, displaying, anti-counterfeiting, and so on.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors are unable or have chosen not to specify which data has been used.

ce:section.ce:para: Color patterns play an indispensable role in the process of world civilization [1–5]. From time immemorial, numerous color patterns have been constructed for applications in different fields [6–9]. Especially, the dynamic patterns, whose colors or shapes can vary under stimulus, have fostered the development of display, anti-counterfeiting, information encryption, and so on [10–13]. Aiming at enriching patterns, endless endeavors have been devoted to developing copious pigments for the pattern elements [14–17]. Among them, photonic crystals (PhC), which are periodic physical nanostructures with photonic band gap (PBG), stand out due to their stability and immunity from any photobleaching and fluorescent background [18–21]. The PBG can modulate light with specific wavelength, leading to striking characteristic structural colors [22–25]. Notably, by integrating PhC with responsive hydrogels, they can serve as an eminent candidate for constructing dynamic patterns [26–29]. The colors of these PhC-derived hydrogel patterns can be easily changed by tailoring the spatial periodicity, imparting these patterns with a variety of colors [30–34]. Although with much progress, the practical applications of these PhC-derived hydrogel patterns were usually limited due to their single modes of color variations. Thus, insights regarding how to enrich the mode of color variation might advance the development of the PhC-derived hydrogel patterns.

In this paper, inspired by the chameleons, we proposed a novel kind of pigment particles with dual mode of color variations (PDCVs) for constructing dynamic patterns, as schemed in Fig. 1 . In nature, the color variation of chameleons has fascinated great scientific attention [35–37]. It’s reported that there are two main mechanisms synergistically responsible for their fast and complex color variations [38–40]. One is based on the dispersion/aggregation of the pigments inside dermal chromatophores of chameleons, which can lead to the pigment color changes [38,39]. The other is caused by adjusting the lattice of guanine nanocrystals inside the dermal iridophores of chameleons, which can modulate the light refraction of their structural colors [35]. Inspired by either of these two modes, researchers have developed a host of optical materials with distinctive features for applications [41–44]. However, it remains alluring but unmet to mimic both of these two color variation modes in one entity.

Herein, we integrated hydrophobic carbon dots (HCDs) into responsive hydrogel PhC particles for generation of the PDCVs. The HCDs, which presented red aggregation-induced emission in water and blue dispersed emission in alcohol, were synthesized through one-pot solvothermal treatment. PDCVs were obtained by utilizing HCDs-tagged responsive hydrogel to replicate the nanostructure of spherical PhC particles, which were assembled by silica nanoparticles in microfluidic droplets. Due to the presence of HCDs, the resultant PDCVs exhibited a reversible fluorescence color variation from red to blue with its solvent changing. Meanwhile, as this solvent changing process could lead to a reversible volume change of the hydrogel, the PDCVs were also imparted with a corresponding structural color variation with wide viewing angles. Taking advantages of these properties, we have demonstrated that the patterns of PDCVs enabled reversible dual color variation and exhibited outstanding performance in information encryption with convenient and fast decryption, indicating great potential in other fields including display, anti-counterfeiting, and so on.

In a typical experiment, the HCDs were generated through one-pot solvothermal approach of the mixture solution containing dithiosalicylic acid, melamine, and acetic acid, as shown in Fig. 2 a. Notably, this amidation and carbonization process removed the hydrophilic groups, thus resulting in the hydrophobic property of HCDs. Aiming at preparing the HCDs for later crosslinking with gels, we then modified the HCDs with rotatable symmetric surfaces according to the previous study [17]. To be specific, the HCDs were vinyl-functionalized by a ring-opening reaction with the help of glycidyl methacrylate (GMA) to obtain vinyl-modified HCDs (VHCDs). The resultant VHCDs powders were acquired after purification and drying via vacuum (Fig. S1). For assuring the modification of vinyl groups on the VHCDs, we compared the Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrums of HCDs with that of VHCDs. Results showed that a new band appeared at 1720 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of VHCDs, which was resulted from the C = O of GMA, indicating the successful modification of vinyl groups on the HCDs (Fig. 2b-c). Furthermore, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was utilized to characterize VHCDs (Fig. 2d). Subsequently, these VHCDs powders were dispersed in ethanol and water, respectively. As presented in Fig. 2e-f, the solution of VHCDs dispersed in ethanol was transparent, displaying blue fluorescence (about 455 nm) under ultraviolet (UV) exposure. In contrast, the solution of VHCDs dispersed in water was turbid, presenting red emission (about 610 nm) under UV exposure. This turbid phenomenon was because that the hydrophobic groups in water drove the VHCDs to aggregate, forming π − π stacking interaction, which quenched the blue emission and turned on red aggregation-induced emission. Overall, we have synthesized VHCDs with red aggregation-induced emission and blue dispersion emission.

For generating the VHCDs-tagged hydrogel, the VHCDs were then added to the mixture of acrylic acid (AAc), acrylamide (AAm), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (Bis) and azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) in the dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) to prepare the pre-gel solution. This pre-gel solution could be solidified to VHCDs-tagged gels covalently linked through free radical copolymerization (Fig. 3 a, Fig. S2a). The resultant gels exhibited reversible fluorescence variation from blue to red when the solvent replaced from ethanol to water due to the dispersion/aggregation of inner VHCDs, serving as ideal gel for fabricating novel PDCVs (Fig. S2b). Then, we employed the pre-gel to replicate the architecture of PhC particles, which were derived from self-assembly of silica nanoparticles in the microfluidic droplets. More explicitly, dried PhC particles were immersed in the pre-gel solution, allowing the pre-gel solution to fill the nanovoids inside the PhC particles. The pre-gel solution in the nanovoids was solidified through free radical copolymerization. The PDCVs were finally obtained by soaking these hybrid PhC particles in the hydrofluoric acid to remove the silica templates. Scanning electronic microscope (SEM) was utilized to record the nanostructures during the process. As shown in Fig. 3b, the original PhC particles possessed periodical internal nanostructure in hexagonal alignment. Fig. 3c indicated that the pre-gel had successfully fulfilled nanovoids among silica nanoparticles. After removing the templates, the final PDCVs possessed highly ordered inverse opal nanostructures (Fig. 3d).

Benefiting from the highly ordered periodic nanostructure, the PhC particles were endowed with vivid structure colors. It’s ascribed to the presence of PBG, which can modulate the reflection of lights in specific wavelength. The wavelength could be calculated via Bragg's equation:
λ
=
1.633 D
n
average
2
- c o
s 2
θ
1 / 2
here D refers to the center distance between the adjacent nanoparticles, naverage represents average refractive index of the materials and θ refers to the Bragg glancing angle of incidence of the light. For PhC particles, the naverage was kept the same and the θ was generally fixed at 90°. Thus, PhC particles with various structure color could be fabricated by deploying silica nanoparticles with different sizes (Fig. 3e and Fig. S3a,b). As the PDCVs were replicated by using PhC particles as templates and thus possessed highly ordered nanostructures, they also displayed striking structure colors (Fig. 3d and Fig. S3c,d). Their spectra of characteristic reflection peaks were recorded in Fig. 3h and Fig. S4. It was worth mentioning that slight red shifts were observed between the resultant PDCVs and their corresponding original PhC particles templates, which was ascribed to the change of d and naverage. Considering the PAAm polymer gels could swell a lot in water due to the electrostatic repulsion among the chains, which might result in an overlarge d, thus making the structure color out of the visible light range, we added poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylate (PEGDA), which has been proved to be effective in inhibiting the swelling of PAAm networks [45], into the pre-gel solution to minimize the swelling ability. It was found that the swelling ratio of gel decreased with the increase of PEGDA concentration (Fig. 3i). Notably, when the concentration of PEGDA was above 5%, the swelling ratio tended to be balanced, approaching 105%. Thus, 5% PEGDA was chosen for fabricating the PDCVs in the following experiments. The mechanical property of PDCVs was characterized, as presented in Fig. S5.

We then tested the color variation property of our PDCVs. Our PDCVs possessed an initial red aggregated-induced emission under UV irradiation because they were exposed to water environment at last fabrication procedure (Fig. 3g). After immersing in ethanol, the water gradually displaced from the hydrogel to allow ethanol intake. The inner aggregated VHCDs of PDCVs tended to disperse due to the solvent exchange, exhibiting a red to blue fluorescence variation from surface to center, as recorded in Fig. 4 a. In addition, the PDCVs were exposed to ethanol/water mixture with different ratio. Their emission spectra in ethanol/water mixture with ratio ranging from 0% to 90% were recorded (Fig. 4c). It was found that the intensity of red emission decreased and that of blue emission enhanced with the increasement of ethanol fraction in the solvent, which was due to the state transformation of VHCDs from aggregation to dispersion. Notably, the fluorescence variation of our PDCVs was reversible. When the water fraction increased again, the PDCVs could recover the red emission. It was found that these pigments exhibited stable fluorescence variation from red to blue in five water–ethanol solvents replacement cycles due to the reversible aggregation/dispersion of the VHCDs (Fig. S6a).

In the meanwhile, the water diffusion and ethanol intake also led to the deswelling of the gel, which was ascribed to the elimination of electrostatic repulsion among the chains. As depicted in Fig. S6b, the volume of PDCVs gradually decreased when the ethanol fraction increased from 0 to 90%. Such volume change was conductive to the structure color variation because of its influence on d. As presented in Fig. 4b and Fig. 4d, the characteristic structure colors of these PDCVs varied gradually from red to green to blue with the increasement of ethanol fraction in the solvent. Due to the reversible volume change process of the gel, the structure color also showed reversible variations in five cycled solvent replacement (Fig. S7). To summarize, our PDCVs possessed dual color variations including fluorescence variation resulted from aggregation/dispersion of VHCDs and structure color variation derived from the volume change in the process of solvent exchange. Subsequently, we utilized these PDCVs as pixels to construct patterns with the help of a PDMS substrate with hole arrays, as depicted in Fig. S8. For constructing diverse patterns, mask with specific shape was employed. By scraping the PDCVs through the PDMS substrate covered with mask, the PDCVs could fill the exposed holes to form desired pattern arrays. Notably, benefiting from the spherical morphology of these PDCVs, the Bragg glancing angle of incidence of the light is the same from any angle [46]. Thus, the color of the patterns was angle-independent, which endowed these patterns with wide viewing angles (Fig. S9).

Taking advantage of the fluorescence variation, the PDCVs could be employed as pixels to fabricate fluorescent dynamic patterns. The PDCVs were filled into the specific holes in the PDMS substrate. The other holes were filled with Rhodamine B (RhB)-contained AAm particles. Notably, the RhB-contained AAm particles was obtained by using the abovementioned pre-gel solution without VHCDs but mixed with RhB molecules to replicate the PhC particles. As shown in Fig. 5 , the pattern array turned on all red under UV irradiation. After exposing the array to ethanol, the aggregated VHCDs dispersed during solvent replacement process, resulting in the fluorescence variation of from red to blue (Fig. 5b). In contrast, the RhB-contained AAm particles maintained its red fluorescence (Fig. 5c). Thus, the all red pattern was changed to a “four leaf clover” pattern. This fluorescence variation behavior indicated PDCVs could serve as ideal pigments for constructing patterns for anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

In addition, we also proved the value of the structure color variation in constructing dynamic colors. As shown in Fig. 6 , the PDCVs were arranged in specific holes in the PDMS patterns and the other holes were filled with PEGDA-derived hydrogel PhC particles. At the beginning, the pattern array possessed all red structure color. After being exposed to ethanol solution, the PDCVs shrank, leading to a blue shift of the structure color. As the attendance of ethanol had little influence on the volume of PEGDA-derived hydrogel PhC particles, their structure colors were kept the same as the initial state. Based on the color difference, the specific patterns were presented. Taking advantage of the property that the PDCVs displayed different structure colors in solvent with different ethanol fraction, dynamic patterns with “M”, “O” and “C” were presented (Fig. 6b-e). In summary, both fluorescence variation and structure color variation of PDCVs could serve as powerful tools for constructing dynamic patterns.

The dual color variation of these PDCVs was speculated to exert a profound impact in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption. To demonstrate, we used these PDCVs to design a more complex dynamic pattern for information encryption, as depicted in Fig. 7 a. The PDCVs, RhB-contained AAm particles and PEGDA-derived hydrogel PhC particles were employed as pixels and arranged in the way as shown in Fig. S10. The pattern initially showed red optical color, which could be observed by naked eyes (Fig. 7b, e). After being exposed to the ethanol solution, part of the pixels displayed green color, which was ascribed to the volume shrinkage of these PDCVs. The pattern turned on a code number of “609880” (Fig. 7c, f). Then, the pattern was exposed to UV irradiation and a code of “573PHC” was presented due to the fluorescence variation of PDCVs (Fig. 7d, g). Notably, if you don’t know the decoding approach, all you get is a pattern with pure color. The final code can only be readout after exposing the pattern to ethanol, followed by UV irradiation. These results verified that the dual color variation of our PDCVs enabled complex information encryption with convenient and fast decryption method, indicating great potential in practical applications.

In conclusion, we have proposed a novel kind of pigments with fluorescence variation and structure color variation. We synthesized VHCDs with dispersed blue emission and aggregated-induced red emission. The PDCVs were fabricated by employing VHCDs-composed gel to replicate the structure of spherical PhC particles. Due to the reversible aggregation/dispersion of VHCDs in water/ethanol, the PDCVs possessed reversible fluorescence variation in the solvent exchange process. Furthermore, the solvent exchange from water to ethanol could lead to a shrinkage of the PDCVs, resulting in the structure color variation. Benefiting from the spherical morphology of these PDCVs, patterns derived from these pigments were endowed with wide viewing angles. It was proved that both the fluorescence variation and structure color variation could be employed to construct various dynamic patterns. Furthermore, the combination of the dual color variation exhibited prominent performance in complex information encryption with convenient and fast decryption method. Thus, we envisioned these novel pigments could usher in a new era for fields including display, anti- counterfeiting, information encryption and so on.

Ethanol, acetic acid, hexyl hydride, dimethyl sulfoxide(DMSO) and hydrofluoric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Acrylic acid (AAc) and N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide(Bis) and Acrylamide (AAm) were obtained via Sigma-Aldrich. Melamine and azodiisobutyronitrile(AIBN) were brought from Macklin Inc. 2,2′-dithiodibenzoic acid and glycidyl methacrylate(GMA) were from Maya-R Inc. and Aladdin Industrial Corporation, respectively. Silicone oil was from Shin-Etsu Chemical Co. Ltd. Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) was brought from Trademark of the Dow Chemical Company. Silica nanoparticles were self-prepared.

The VCDs were synthesized through typical one-pot solvothermal treatment. First, 2,2′-dithiodibenzoic acid (1.088 g) and melamine (0.403 g) were dispersed in acetic acid (80 mL) by ultrasonic dispersion, followed by transferring the mixture to a Teflon reactor. Then, the reactor was put in an air oven at 180 °C for 10 h. The resultant solution was cooled to room temperature and mixed with boiling water for 6 h to wash away the residual raw solvent and materials. After cooling down, vacuum filtration was utilized to collect the resultant HCDs. The HCDs were stored in dry at room temperature.

For obtaining VHCDs, the resultant HCDs (2 g) were firstly dissolved in DMSO. Then, glycidyl methacrylate (20 mL) were added and the mixture was kept stirring at 25 °C for 24 h. Subsequently, the mixture was mixed with boiling water for 6 h to wash away the residual raw solvent and materials. Finally, the VHCDs were obtained by using vacuum filtration.

The PhC particles were generated through a co-flow microfluidic approach. To be specific, silicone oil (50CS) and silica nanoparticles-dispersed aqueous solution (20 wt%) were selected as outer phase and inner phase, respectively. 0.5 mL/h and 3 mL/h were set as the flow velocity for inner and outer phase, respectively. The outer phase cut the inner phase into droplets containing silica nanoparticles. Then, the droplets were collected in a plastic box with flat bottom containing silicone oil (500CS). Next, the box was kept at 80 °C for 12 h to allow the evaporation of inner water and self-assembly of the silica nanoparticles. After that, the silicone oil was removed by soaking the particles in the hexyl hydride. Finally, the particles were settled in muffle furnace(800 °C,12 h) to obtain PhC particles.

To fabricate PDCVs, the pre-gel solution was prepared in advance by mixing AAm (600 mg), AAc (0.15 mL), Bis (8 mg), AIBN (1 mg) and VHCDs(20 mg) with DMSO (3 mL) according to the previous work17. Then, the dried PhC particles were soaked in the pre-gel solution for 2 h for ensuring the successful filling of the inner nanovoids. Then, the mixture was kept at 80 °C for 12 h to solidify the pre-gel solution. Next, the residual gel in the surface of PhC particles was removed via soft rubbing with fingers. After that, these hybrid particles were immersed in hydrofluoric acid to remove the silica templates. Finally, the PDCVs were obtained by washing them with pure water for 5 times to remove the residual hydrofluoric acid.

The PDMS substrate with hole array was fabricated by using prepolymer of PDMS to replicate the positive template with convex pillar array. Notably, the diameter of pillar was smaller than that of PDCVs. Then, the PDMS substrate was covered with mask in a specific shape. To construct the patterns, the pigments were scrapped into the holes with the help of glass scraper, followed by moving away the mold. Notably, by using other mask and repeating the above operation, arrays with desired patterns could be obtained.

Optical images were taken via a stereomicroscope (JSZ6S, Jiangnan novel optics) equipping with a charge coupled device (CCD) camera (Oplenic digital camera). All the SEM was characterized by a field emission SEM (FESEM, Ultra Plus, Zeiss). The fluorescent images were captured by a microscope (Olympus IX71) with a CCD (Olympus DP30BW). The spectra were measured by employing a fiber-optic spectrometer (Halogen Light Source).
Y. Zhao conceived the idea and designed the experiment. L. Cai conducted experiments and data analysis, and wrote the manuscript. Y. Wang, D. Xu, and H. Chen helped the manuscript writing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFA0908200), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (T2225003, 52073060 and 61927805), the Nanjing Medical Science and Technique Development Foundation (ZKX21019), the Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2021B1515120054), and the Shenzhen Fundamental Research Program (JCYJ20190813152616459 and JCYJ20210324133214038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.142000.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Colouration by total internal reflection and interference at microscale concave interfaces

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An abstract drawing from the 73,000-year-old levels at Blombos Cave, South Africa

Innes C. Cuthill, William L. Allen, Kevin Arbuckle, Barbara Caspers, George Chaplin, Mark E. Hauber, Geoffrey E. Hill, Nina G. Jablonski, Chris D.
